structure.title: Configuration 56
structure.summary: The "Configuration 56" section outlines two key components: **10.1 Default Values**, which specify predefined initial settings for system parameters, and **10.2 Semantic Constraints**, which define rules governing the validity and structure of configuration data. Default values ensure consistent baseline configurations, while semantic constraints enforce logical and syntactic correctness in configuration definitions. Both subsections are critical for ensuring proper system initialization and validation. The section focuses on foundational aspects of configuration management without introducing additional parameters or exceptions.

Introduction and functional overview
structure.summary: The specification outlines the functionality, API, and configuration for the FC Firewall, which manages and configures the host-based firewall on the ECU hosting the Adaptive Platform. It configures the underlying firewall engine based on Firewall Rule configurations deployed via manifests. The FC Firewall provides runtime interfaces to dynamically adapt firewall rules for

structure.title: Acronyms and Abbreviations
structure.summary: The "Acronyms and Abbreviations" section provides a glossary specific to the `FC Firewall`, listing terms relevant only to its context. It notes that a broader general list of acronyms and abbreviations is available in the [1, ө AUTOSAR glossary]. The section does not include terms outside the scope of the `FC Firewall`. All technical details are confined to the firewall's documentation. No additional information beyond the referenced glossary is provided.

structure.title: Related documentation
structure.summary: The document outlines the software specification for the FC Firewall. It is complemented by two related documents: **RS_Firewall** [2], which details the requirement specification for the AUTOSAR firewall at the Foundation level, and **TPS_ManifestSpecification** [3], which defines the Adaptive AUTOSAR MetaModel and includes modeling of the FC Firewall. These documents provide foundational and architectural context for the FC Firewall's implementation. All referenced materials are critical for understanding the specification's scope and technical requirements.

nodes.title: Input documents & related standards and norms
Further applicable specification
nodes.summary: The FC Firewall adheres to the AUTOSAR core specification [4]. The chapter "General requirements for all FunctionalClusters" from this specification is an additional and mandatory requirement for implementing the FC Firewall. This chapter must be considered as part of the implementation guidelines to ensure compliance with the specification. The reference to [4] indicates the specific AUTOSAR core specification document applicable to the FC Firewall. The requirement emphasizes that the chapter is both supplementary and essential for the firewall's design and implementation.

nodes.title: Known limitations
nodes.summary: The "Known limitations" section states that two features are not supported in this release: (1) firewall rule (de-)activation during runtime, and (2) support for OEM-defined SEVs. These limitations indicate specific functionalities are unavailable, with the first related to dynamic firewall management and the second tied to hardware-assisted virtualization extensions defined by original equipment manufacturers. No additional details or workarounds are provided in the text.

structure.title: Dependencies to other Functional Clusters
structure.summary: AUTOSAR avoids standardizing platform-level interfaces between Functional Clusters to enable efficient, OS-dependent implementations. This section outlines the Firewall's interaction with other Functional Clusters, detailing provided interfaces (Section 5.1) and required interfaces (Section 5.2). The goal is to define boundaries and interactions without specifying syntactic details, ensuring compatibility across documents and enabling parallel development

structure.title: A detailed technical architecture documentation of the overall AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform is provided in [5].

nodes.title: Required Interfaces
nodes.summary: The **Firewall** requires two interfaces from other Functional Clusters within the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. The **ExecutionClient** interface is provided by the **Execution Management** cluster, and the **EventReporter** interface is provided by the **Intrusion Detection System Manager** cluster. The **EventReporter** interface is specifically used by the Firewall to report standardized security events. The initial table in the section incorrectly states "No provided interfaces," but the subsequent table

nodes.title: Architecture Overview
nodes.summary: The `FC Firewall` acts as a management cluster abstracting the underlying firewall engine, which can be implemented via OS-level TCP/IP inspection, hardware

nodes.title: Network packet inspection
nodes.summary: The FC Firewall enforces network packet inspection via **FirewallRules** in the AUTOSAR methodology, which define expected packet patterns and associated actions. It processes each packet by sequentially comparing it against ordered **FirewallRules** (configured via Metamodel) until the first pattern match, at which point the corresponding action is executed. Inspection mechanisms include **stateless** (header field checks), **stateful** (protocol state transitions), and **deep packet inspection** (application-layer protocols like SOME/IP, DDS, DoIP), with pattern definitions spanning OSI layers (DataLinkLayerRule, NetworkLayerRule, etc.) and aggregated into **FirewallRule**. For traffic direction, **ingress** and **egress** inspections apply only to rules referenced by **FirewallRuleProps

nodes.title: Stateless packet inspection
nodes.summary: Stateless packet inspection involves the firewall examining network protocol headers up to OSI layer 4 and comparing them against predefined expected values in FirewallRules. It evaluates DataLinkLayerRule, NetworkLayerRule, and TransportLayerRule configurations to determine matches based on exact header field values. Ranges for parameters like IP addresses or ports can be defined via subnets or min/max thresholds, with matching logic applied to addresses or values within those ranges. Unconfigured parameters in the Metamodel are ignored, and checksum verification is performed for specific protocols if enabled.

nodes.title: Inspection of not modeled protocols
nodes.summary: The _FC Firewall_ supports stateless packet inspection for modeled protocols (Ethernet, IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, TCP, UDP) and allows adding unmodeled protocols via two mechanisms. **EtherType inspection** identifies protocols at the data link layer using IEEE 802.3 EtherType values, enabling blocking via `DataLinkLayerRule

nodes.title: Stateful packet inspection
nodes.summary: Stateful packet inspection in the FC Firewall involves monitoring TCP state transitions, enforcing timeout checks, limiting parallel TCP sessions, and validating TCP statemachine compliance with RFC 793. The firewall uses specific EtherType/Protocol pairs (e.g., ARP, SRP, MACsec) to identify packet types during inspection

nodes.title: Deep packet inspection
nodes.summary: The firewall supports deep packet inspection (DPI) of application layer protocols to analyze network packets. It specifically inspects SOME/IP (including SOME/IP-SD), DDS, DoIP, and Generic deep packet inspection protocols. This capability enables detailed examination of protocol-specific data for enhanced security and traffic management. The inclusion of SOME/IP-SD highlights support for extended service discovery in SOME/IP. The listed protocols cover automotive, distributed systems, and generic network traffic analysis.

nodes.title: SOME/IP
nodes.summary: The FC Firewall inspects SOME/IP header fields (e.g., Service ID, Method ID) for access control, enabling granular service-level filtering. Multiple SOME/IP messages may be encapsulated in a single TCP frame, but each requires a dedicated FirewallRule, as the metamodel limits aggregation to one message per rule. Length verification is supported via `SomeipProtocolRule.lengthVerification`, comparing TCP payload size to the combined length of included messages. For SOME/IP-SD, similar logic applies, with rules

nodes.title: DDS
nodes.summary: The section outlines three requirements (AP_SWS_Fw_30020, AP_SWS_Fw_30021, AP_SWS_Fw_m_30022) under the upstream requirement FO_RS_Fw_00003. It specifies that a **SomeipSdRule** matches network packets if their values fall within a defined range of minor or major versions, as specified by **SomeipSdRule.minMinorVersion**, **SomeipSdRule.maxMinorVersion**, **SomeipSdRule.minMajorVersion**, or **SomeipSdRule.maxMajorVersion**. The range includes all values between the minimum and maximum, inclusive. The text emphasizes that these rules are part of the upstream requirements for firmware implementation. No additional details about the rules' application or exceptions are provided.

nodes.title: Allowlists and Blocklists
nodes.summary: Firewalls are categorized into **Allowlist** (explicitly allows defined traffic, blocks everything else) and **Blocklist** (explicitly blocks defined traffic, allows everything else) types. The action for a matched **FirewallRule** is determined by **FirewallRuleProps.action**, which specifies whether to allow or block the packet. If no rule matches, the firewall defaults to **StateDependentFirewall.defaultAction** (block/allow). The system supports **mixed Allow/Blocklist firewalls**, enabling both explicit blocking and allowing rules, which facilitates pattern matching for IDS event mapping (see Sec.

nodes.title: Rate limiting
nodes.summary: The firewall employs rate limiting via pattern matching to detect off-frequency cyclic messages, such as those from man-in-the-middle attacks or faulty ECUs. It uses the leaky bucket algorithm, implemented in both software (FC Firewall) and supported by certain hardware. When **FirewallRule.bucketSize** and **FirewallRule.refillAmount** are configured, the firewall tracks pattern matches by incrementing a counter for each match and decrementing it at the rate defined by **refillAmount**. If the counter exceeds **bucketSize** during a pattern match, the network packet is dropped.

nodes.title: Firewall Rule Management
nodes.summary: The **FirewallRule Management** section outlines two methods for managing **FirewallRules** in the **FC Firewall**: OTA updates via **UCM** and runtime (de-)activation of rules. OTA updates are the preferred method for adding, modifying, or removing rules, especially for new applications, as they are deployed alongside software via UCM. A runtime interface allows dynamic activation/deactivation of existing rules for real-time threat mitigation, but this feature is not supported in the current release. Firewall configurations, including rules

nodes.title: Functional cluster life-cycle
nodes.summary: The Functional Cluster life-cycle section describes that the FC Firewall's initialization and deinitialization are managed via `ara::core::Initialize` and `ara::core::Deinitialize`. Applications must use these functions to properly set up and tear down the FC Firewall. No FC Firewall APIs should be invoked before `ara::core::Initialize` is called or after `ara::core::Deinitialize` is executed, as this is explicitly prohibited. The text emphasizes strict adherence to this lifecycle to ensure correct operation.

nodes.title: Startup
nodes.summary: The "Startup" section outlines that upon calling `ara::core::initialize`, the FC Firewall must read manifest information and prepare access structures for communication. These structures facilitate interaction between application processes and stack processes, or other resources required by the firewall. The requirement is tied to the upstream specification FO_RS_Fw_00010. Access structures may include communication channels or additional resources necessary for firewall operations. The process ensures the firewall is properly configured to manage application interactions.

nodes.title: Shutdown
nodes.summary: When the `ara::core::Deinitialize` function is invoked, the FC Firewall is required to close all acquired handles and free all associated access structures, as specified by the upstream requirement FO_RS_Fw_00010. This ensures proper resource release during shutdown. The action is triggered explicitly by calling the `Deinitialize` method. All handles and access structures must be explicitly closed and freed to prevent resource leaks. The process adheres to the upstream requirement's specifications without additional details.

nodes.title: Security Events
nodes.summary: Firewalls are integral to Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) by monitoring network traffic to detect attacks in vehicle networks. AUTOSAR's IdsM (IDS Manager) aggregates and qualifies security events from IDS sensors, forwarding them to either persistent memory or the vehicle-central IdsR. The FC Firewall acts as an IDS sensor, raising security events (SEvs) to the IdsM. It defines a set of SEvs (see Sec. 7.6.1.1) and specifies conditions for triggering them (see Sec. 7.6.1.2).

nodes.title: Raising SEvs
nodes.summary: The FC Firewall raises SEvs (Security Events) only when a network packet is blocked by the default action (no matching FirewallRule), as the second case (rule match with action) is deferred to a future release. SEvs are relevant only if the default action is to block unspecified packets. To identify the mismatched protocol, the firewall calculates the least distance by evaluating FirewallRules from the lowest ISO OSI Layer upward, selecting the protocol on the next non-matching layer. Specific SEvs are raised based on the identified protocol (e.g., Ethernet, IPv4, IPv6, ICMP) as defined in upstream requirements. The example illustrates that rules with the

structure.title: Service Interfaces
structure.summary: The "Service Interfaces" section states that the FC Firewall has no provided or required service interfaces. This indicates that the firewall does not expose any APIs, protocols, or interface definitions for external interaction or integration. The absence of service interfaces implies that the firewall operates as a standalone component without inter-process communication or external service dependencies. No additional technical details or exceptions are mentioned regarding service interfaces. The documentation explicitly confirms the lack of both provided and required interfaces.

structure.title: Configuration
structure.summary: The **FC Firewall** configuration structure is defined in the **TPS_Manifest** under the **AdaptiveFirewallModuleInstantiation** section. This chapter establishes default values and semantic constraints for the configuration model. It provides a framework for defining and enforcing configuration parameters. The details ensure consistency and correctness in firewall setup. All technical specifications are preserved as described.

structure.title: Mentioned Manifest Elements
structure.summary: The section outlines meta-classes related to the Adaptive Firewall in the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform, marked with `atp.Status=candidate`. **AdaptiveFirewallModuleInstantiation** defines firewall configuration attributes for a machine, inheriting from multiple bases and aggregating `stateDep Firewall` rules. **AdaptiveFirewallToPortPrototypeMapping** links the firewall module to RPortPrototype via a FirewallModeSwitchInterface, with attributes referencing processes and port prototypes. **DataLinkLayerRule** configures filter rules for the DataLink layer, specifying MAC addresses, EtherType, VLAN ID, and priority. A truncated **DdsRule** class is also mentioned, likely for DDS firewall rule configuration. All classes are exclusive to the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform.
